title: Cat Scratching Ears Until Raw: Causes, Urgency, and Care Guide
meta_desc: If your cat is scratching their ears until raw, it could be mites, allergies, or infection. Learn why this is a medium-urgency concern and how to help.
content_html:
What is it?

When a cat scratches their ears to the point of causing raw skin, bleeding, or hair loss, it is a clinical sign of intense pruritus (itching). This is not just a habit; it is a physical response to significant irritation. Common causes include ear mites, bacterial or yeast infections, flea infestations, or allergic reactions to food or environmental triggers. The act of scratching creates open wounds, which can lead to painful secondary skin infections.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While it is generally not life-threatening in the immediate sense, your cat is in a high level of discomfort and pain. You should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours. If you notice the ear flap becoming swollen and pillowy (an aural hematoma), a foul odor, or if your cat is tilting their head and acting disoriented, you should prioritize an appointment as soon as possible to prevent permanent damage to the ear canal.

Why a photo helps triage

Capturing a clear, well-lit photo of the ear and the surrounding raw skin can be vital for triage. A photo allows a veterinary professional to assess the type of discharge—such as dark, crumbly debris indicative of mites or yellowish discharge suggestive of infection—and the severity of the tissue damage. This information helps the clinical team determine how quickly your cat needs to be seen and prepares them for the specific diagnostic tests required upon your arrival.

faqs.question: Can I put over-the-counter creams on my cat's raw ears?
faqs.answer: It is best to avoid applying any creams or ointments until a veterinarian has examined the ear. Some human medications are toxic to cats, and certain products can cause further irritation or mask the symptoms, making a diagnosis more difficult.

faqs.question: Could my cat's diet be causing the ear itching?
faqs.answer: Yes, food allergies are a common cause of chronic ear inflammation in cats. If the itching persists after treating for mites or infections, your vet may recommend a hypoallergenic diet trial.

faqs.question: How can I stop my cat from scratching until the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: The most effective way to prevent further self-trauma is to use an Elizabethan collar (the 'cone of shame'). This provides a physical barrier that allows the raw skin to begin healing while you wait for professional treatment.
